coredata.dc:title: Gold nanoclusters/graphene quantum dots complex-based dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescence probe for the determination of glucose
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Hong, Guo-Lin
Deng, Hao-Hua
Zhao, Hai-Ling
Zou, Zhi-Yan
Huang, Kai-Yuan
Peng, Hua-Ping
Liu, Yin-Huan
Chen, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report the design of a single-excitation/double-emission ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor for the determination of glucose. The sensing system combines glucose oxidation catalyzed by glucose oxidase, Fenton chemistry, Fe3+-sensitive fluorescent gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), and Fe3+-inert fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs). We used orange-fluorescent AuNCs co-modified with bovine serum albumin and 3-mercaptopropionic acid as the indicator probe, and GQDs with the same excitation wavelength as the BSA/MPA-AuNCs, but with different emission wavelength, as the reference probe. The fluorescence intensity-ratio between 420 nm and 575 nm (F420/F575) was used to quantitatively determine glucose with a low detection limit of 0.18 μM, and the nanosensor was successfully used to detect glucose in human serum. This ratiometric fluorescence sensing system, based on AuNCs and GQDs, ensures sensitive and convenient determination of glucose, and has broad application prospects for biomedical-analysis applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Gold nanoclusters
Graphene quantum dots
Hydrogen peroxide
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The ratiometric fluorescence sensing system: feasibility and principles

National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustrating glucose sensing with the GQDs/AuNC/Fe2+ system.

(A) Fluorescence excitation spectra of (a) GQDs and (b) AuNCs, and the emission spectra of (c) GQDs and (d) AuNCs. (B) Influence of detector voltage on the F575 and F420/F575 values. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture of GQDs, AuNCs, and Fe2+ in the (a) absence and (b) presence of H2O2. (D) Calibration curve of F420/F575 as a function of H2O2 concentration. Inset: Photographic image showing fluorescence color changes undergone by the GQD/AuNC/Fe2+ system with increasing H2O2 concentration (0–15 μM) when irradiated by UV light (302 nm).

(A) Ratiometric fluorescence signals (F420/F575) of (a) GQDs/AuNCs/Fe2+, (b) GQDs/AuNCs/Fe2+ + glucose, (c) GQDs/AuNCs/Fe2+ + GOx, (d) GQDs/AuNCs/Fe2+ + glucose + GOx, and (e) GQDs/AuNCs + glucose + GOx. (B) F420/F575 of GQDs/AuNCs + glucose + GOx as a function of Fe2+ concentration.
(A) F420/F575 as a function of reaction time for GOx-catalyzed glucose oxidation. (B) Kinetic curve for the response of GQDs/AuNCs/Fe2+ to H2O2.

(A) Calibration curve of ratiometric fluorescence intensity (F420/F575) as a function of glucose concentration. (B) Ratiometric fluorescence responses of the GQD/AuNC/Fe2+ nanosystem to different interferents. Samples marked with 0-13 corresponding to blank, glucose, maltose, fructose, lactose, KCl, NaH2PO4, CaCl2, MgCl2, ZnSO4, urea, lactate, creatinine, and creatine, respectively. The concentration of glucose was 15 μM, and that of other interferents was 300 μM.
Glucose concentrations in human sera determined by the colorimetric method (y-axis) against those determined by ratiometric fluorescence (x-axis).
Comparing the performance of reported glucose fluorescence nanosensors.
The recovery of standard additions of glucose in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Gold nanoclusters/graphene quantum dots complex-based dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescence probe for the determination of glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Laboratory Medicine, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Laboratory Medicine, The Affiliated Fuzhou Second Hospital of Xiamen University, Fuzhou 350007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Affiliated Fuzhou Second Hospital of Xiamen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Laboratory Medicine, the Affiliated Fuzhou Second Hospital of Xiamen University, Fuzhou 350007, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report the design of a single-excitation/double-emission ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor for the determination of glucose. The sensing system combines glucose oxidation catalyzed by glucose oxidase, Fenton chemistry, Fe3+-sensitive fluorescent gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), and Fe3+-inert fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs). We used orange-fluorescent AuNCs co-modified with bovine serum albumin and 3-mercaptopropionic acid as the indicator probe, and GQDs with the same excitation wavelength as the BSA/MPA-AuNCs, but with different emission wavelength, as the reference probe. The fluorescence intensity-ratio between 420 nm and 575 nm (F420/F575) was used to quantitatively determine glucose with a low detection limit of 0.18 μM, and the nanosensor was successfully used to detect glucose in human serum. This ratiometric fluorescence sensing system, based on AuNCs and GQDs, ensures sensitive and convenient determination of glucose, and has broad application prospects for biomedical-analysis applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Through the use of dual-fluorescence emission modes that can reduce or even eliminate the impact of non-target factors, ratiometric fluorescence sensors provide built-in correction [1–9]. In addition, the presence of an analyte and its concentration are easily determined with the naked eye by observing changes in luminescent colour, which is another unique feature of the ratiometric fluorescence strategy. Ratiometric fluorescence probes are generally divided into two types according to signal-generation mode; i.e., single-excitation/double-excitation probes and double-excitation/double-emission probes. Among them, the former is more commonly used because two emission signals are generated using the same excitation wavelength and instrument parameters.

The use of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a traditional strategy for constructing single-excitation/double-emission probes [10–13], but the efficiency of FRET depends on the distance between the acceptor and the donor, as well as the donor-emission/acceptor-absorption overlap efficiency. Therefore, ratiometric probes that can work independently on FRET are more attractive. Such probes can be constructed by coupling two fluorescent materials that can be excited at the same wavelength but emit at two resolvable wavelengths. In such a system, the fluorescence of one material is inert to the analyte and is used as the reference signal, while the fluorescence of the other material is sensitive to the analyte and used as the response signal.

As a new type of luminescent material, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) have received wide interest in the biological imaging and sensing fields due to exceptional characteristics including water solubility, high colloidal stability, good biocompatibility, and unique photophysical properties [14–19]. The fluorescence properties of AuNCs depend on the surrounding environment, particle size, and the surface ligand. With this in mind, different targets can be analyzed using a variety of mechanisms, such as metal bonding, ligand dissociation, gold-core etching, induced aggregation, combining recognition groups, and catalytic reactions [20–26]. However, most strategies that can be used to detect single-wavelength emissions, tend to be influenced by the concentration and environment of the probe, the sample matrix, and instrument stability, which limits measurement accuracy. Therefore, the design and development of ratiometric fluorescence nanosensors based on AuNCs is attractive.

In this work, we developed a single-excitation/double-emission probe based on orange-emitting AuNCs co-modified with bovine serum albumin and 3-mercaptopropionic acid, and blue-emitting graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for hydrogen peroxide and glucose sensing. Glucose oxidase catalyzes enzymatic glucose oxidation-reaction, thus produce the H2O2. In the presence of Fe2+, AuNC fluorescence is quenched by Fe3+ generated through Fenton chemistry [27], while GQD fluorescence unaffected. Because variations in fluorescence depend on the concentration of H2O2, the constructed probe is capable of detecting glucose in human serum. Such fluorescence sensor has many advantageous such as highly sensitive, very precise, and detects rapidly. We expect it to be broadly used to detect hydrogen peroxide and glucose in real samples.

Trisodium citrate, bovine serum albumin, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium hydroxide, hydrogen peroxide, phosphoric acid, acetic acid, and glucose were obtained from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chloroauric acid, 3-mercaptopropionic acid, ferrous chloride, and glucose oxidase were purchased from the Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used as the solvent in all experiments. Serum samples were provided by the Affiliated Fuzhou Second Hospital of Xiamen University. All experiments were approved by the Ethics Committee of Affiliated Fuzhou Second Hospital of Xiamen University, Fuzhou, China (No. FSHXU IACUC 1649.092).

AuNCs were synthesized according to the previously reported method [28]. Bovine serum albumin solution (2.5 mL, 50 mg/mL), HAuCl4 solution (2.5 mL, 10 mM), NaOH solution (0.25 mL, 1 M), and 3-mercaptopropionic acid solution (0.25 mL, 4 M) were mixed homogenously and allowed to react at 4 °C for 1 h. The product was dialyzed in phosphate buffer solution (20 mM, pH = 3) for 48 h, and then dialyzed in deionized water for 12 h to obtain pure BSA/MPA-AuNCs. The resulting AuNCs can maintain their optical stability for at least three months.

GQDs were prepared by pyrolysis of trisodium citrate [29]. In a typical process, trisodium citrate was heated at 200 °C for 4 min, after that water was added to the prepared GQDs, with the mixture stirred vigorously for 10 min. The colloidal solution was purified by ultrafiltration and the 3–10-kDa segment was collected for further use. The resulting GQDs can maintain their optical stability for at least three months.

To detect glucose, a probe solution containing GQDs (0.072 mg/mL), AuNCs, and Fe2+ (200 μM) with a volume ratio of 5:3:2 was prepared using a HAc-NaAc buffer solution (50 mM, pH = 3). Glucose solutions (125 μL, pH = 7.4) with various concentrations were mixed with 125 μL of GOx solution (1 U/mL, pH = 7.4), and incubated at 35 °C for 10 min. Then, 1000 μL of the probe solution was added, and the mixture was reacted for 5 min, then the fluorescence intensities at 420 and 575 nm was measured using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer (Agilent, Santa Clara, CA, USA) (excited at 340 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric fluorescence sensing system: feasibility and principles
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the experimental feasibility, we first examined the fluorescence properties of the AuNCs and GQDs. The fluorescence spectra of the GQDs and AuNCs (Fig. 1 A) exhibit maximum excitation wavelengths of 330 and 340 nm, respectively, with corresponding emission peaks observed at 420 and 575 nm, respectively. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescence probe can be established with a single-excitaion wavelength of 340 nm.

The ratiometric fluorescence signal from the GQD/AuNC probe is more stable than the BSA/MPA-AuNC fluorescence. Fig. 1B shows that the intensity of the fluorescence signal from the AuNCs at 575 nm increases as the detector voltage is gradually increased, while the F420/F575 value of the GQD/AuNC probe remains unchanged. The relative standard derivation (RSD) of the F420/F575 values across various detector voltages is 0.8 % (n = 14), which highlights the significant advantage of the ratiometric method over the use of a single-wavelength fluorescence technique.

Fig. 1C shows the fluorescence spectrum of the mixture of GQDs, AuNCs, and Fe2+. The GQD/AuNC/Fe2+ system shows well-separated peaks at 420 and 575 nm that correspond to the fluorescence emissions of the GQDs and AuNCs, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of AuNCs can be hardly influenced by the sole addition of Fe2+ or H2O2 [27]. However, when Fe2+ and H2O2 coexist, the Fe3+ produced by the Fenton reaction quenches the AuNC fluorescence [27], while the fluorescence of the GQDs is almost unaffected. The F420/F575 ratio was observed to increase gradually with increasing H2O2 concentration. The linear H2O2-detection range was found to be 1–15 μM with the regression equation determined to be: F420/F575 = 0.086[H2O2] (μM) + 0.444 (r = 0.998) (Fig. 1D). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.06 μM using the equation LOD = 3S/K, where S represents the standard deviation of the blank signal (0.00166) and K is the slope of the working curve (0.086). The solution gradually underwent an orange to blue color change with increasing H2O2 concentration when irradiated with 302-nm UV light (Fig. 1E); even 1 μM of H2O2 was found to be distinguishable by the naked eye. The sensitivity of the GQD/AuNC/Fe2+ system to H2O2 highlights the great potential of this ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of enzymes and substrates involved in biological processes that produce H2O2.

Glucose oxidase (GOx) specifically catalyzes the oxidation of glucose to gluconic acid. In this process, molecular oxygen, as the electron acceptor, is converted into H2O2; therefore, the biocatalytic oxidation of glucose can be monitored by tracking the effect of H2O2 on the GQD/AuNC/Fe2+ system. Glucose sensing using the developed ratiometric probe was verified by observing by the GQD/AuNC/Fe2+ system respond to GOx, glucose, and the catalytic product. As shown in Fig. 2 A, glucose or GOx alone did not cause significant signal change in the signal from the GQD/AuNC/Fe2+ system. On the other hand, the F420/F575 ratio was observed to increase significantly in the presence of both glucose and GOx, which confirms the feasibility of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor system for the detection of glucose. In addition, the H2O2 generated during the GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose does not induce a signal change in the absence of Fe2+. F420/F575 gradually increased with increasing ferrous-ion concentration, and plateaued at a Fe2+ concentration of 300 μM. The principles involved in the single-excitation/double-emission-mode ratiometric fluorescence detection of glucose using the GQDs/AuNCs are shown in Scheme 1 .

Response time is a very important performance indicator of a glucose sensor; therefore, we examined the time-dependent response of the sensing system. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the maximum fluorescence ratio was obtained when the glucose oxidation reaction was carried out for 10 min in the presence of 1 U/mL GOx; meanwhile, the GQD/AuNC/Fe2+ system required only 5 min to exhibit the maximum ratio in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. 3B). The sensor strategy, with its short operating time, provides significant prospects for practical applications.

The selectivity of the GQD/AuNC ratiometric fluorescence probe was evaluated, and the values of F420/F575 in the presence of glucose and other substances were recorded. As shown in Fig. 4B, all the tested interferents did not induce any obvious signal changes, even when at concentrations 20-times higher than that of glucose, which indicates that this method is highly specific for glucose and that it can be further used in practical bioanalysis applications.

In this work, we constructed a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe by mixing blue-emitting GQDs with orange-emitting AuNCs that share the same excitation characteristics. H2O2 transforms Fe2+ into Fe3+, which quenches the AuNC fluorescence; therefore, this system can be used to monitor the generation of hydrogen peroxide and indirectly determine glucose. The linear detection range of the reported system is 1–15 μM, with a LOD of 0.18 μM. The method was successfully used to determine glucose in human sera, which highlights its practicability. The method can easily be adapted to measure other physiological substances and their corresponding oxidases.

Guo-Lin Hong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Hao-Hua Deng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Hai-Ling Zhao: Methodology, Investigation, Validation. Zhi-Yan Zou: Methodology, Investigation. Kai-Yuan Huang: Methodology, Investigation. Hua-Ping Peng: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Yin-Huan Liu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Wei Chen: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We sincerely acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81772287, 21675024), the Program for Innovative Leading Talents in Fujian Province (2016B016), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in Fujian Province University (2018B033).
